bullets.transcript_excerpt: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explains that in this example dataset, every single one of the five defaulters happened to be wearing a blue shirt, which is purely coincidental. This small sample size makes the pattern meaningless as a predictor.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: The five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews. And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a model trained on this data will also pick up 'blue shirt' as a strong predictive signal, which is a textbook case of overfitting with no causal evidence. Human logic tells us shirt color should not influence default behavior.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicant's default behavior, right? The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts. So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.

ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

training error means the error on the training data set.
And generalization error refers to your error on a new data.

not guarantee a good score on the future exam, where the future exam actually represent the
generalization error, right?
And one student may get zero error on past exam by root learning.
But that student may have a worse score compared to another student who understand the reasons
So even if student B may not have good score on past exam, may not that good at past exam,
So this is the trade-off we have mentioned in your Model 3 class about training and generalization

bullets.ppt_bullet: Training Error and Generalization Error
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduced this as a classic discussion in machine learning. It sets up the core trade-off between how well a model performs on training data versus new, unseen data.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And this introduced our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher defined training error simply as the error measured on the training dataset. It reflects how well the model fits the data it was trained on.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: training error means the error on the training data set.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error is the error on new, previously unseen data. The teacher contrasted it directly with training error.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And generalization error refers to your error on a new data.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher used the analogy of studying for a future exam using past exams to make the concept intuitive. Past exams represent training data, and the future exam represents new, unseen data.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: This is very similar to we make practices for a future exam with past exams.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher emphasized that a low training error does not imply good generalization, using the exam analogy where the future exam represents generalization error. This is the core warning about overfitting.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: For those who do well on past exam, that means you get a very small training error that does not guarantee a good score on the future exam, where the future exam actually represent the generalization error, right?

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
bullets.ai_comment: Student A achieves zero training error through rote memorization, but this approach does not translate to good performance on the future exam. This illustrates overfitting.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And one student may get zero error on past exam by root learning.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Student B understands the reasons for given answers
bullets.ai_comment: Student B, who understands the underlying reasoning rather than memorizing, may score lower on past exams but is expected to generalize better to the future exam. This illustrates better generalization performance.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: But that student may have a worse score compared to another student who understand the reasons... So even if student B may not have good score on past exam, may not that good at past exam... this is the trade-off we have mentioned in your Model 3 class about training and generalization.

as long as he can understand the reasonings, he should be able to have a higher score.
